perturbation.original_text: The receiving party shall protect all of the disclosing party's Proprietary Information as confidential information and, except with the prior written consent of the disclosing party or as otherwise specifically provided herein, shall. not disclose, copy or distribute such Proprietary Information to any other individual, corporation or entity for a period of three (3) years from the date of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party shall protect all of the disclosing party's Proprietary Information as confidential information and, except with the prior written consent of the disclosing party, shall not disclose, copy or distribute such Proprietary Information to any other individual, corporation or entity.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'or as otherwise specifically provided herein' from Section 2.a creates an in-text contradiction. Section 1 originally defines what 'Proprietary Information' is, including exceptions. By removing this clause in Section 2.a, any specific exceptions outlined in Section 1 would no longer apply. This contradicts the intent of Section 1, making the definition of Proprietary Information less clear and enforceable, because section 2.a is not longer referencing that 'Proprietary Information' can be disclosed in specialy given situations described in section 1.
perturbation.contradicted_text: For purposes of this Agreement, "Proprietary Information" shall mean written, documentary or oral information of any kind disclosed by Cyberlux or ICT to the other and designated as proprietary information, including, but not limited to, (a) information of a business, planning, marketing or technical nature, (b) models, tools, hardware and software, and (c) any documents, reports, memoranda, notes, files or analyses prepared by or on behalf of the receiving party that contain, summarize or are based upon any Proprietary Information, provided that "Proprietary Information" shall not include information which: (i) is publicly available prior to the date of this Agreement; (ii) becomes publicly available after the date of this Agreement trough no wrongful act of the receiving party; (iii) is furnished to others by the disclosing party without similar restrictions on their right to use or disclose; (iv) is known by the receiving party without any proprietary restrictions at the time of receipt of such information from the disclosing party or becomes rightfully known to the receiving party without proprietary restrictions from a source other than the disclosing party; (v) is independently developed by the receiving party by persons who did not have access, directly or indirectly, to the proprietary Information; or /1/ (vi) is obligated to be produced under order of a court of competent jurisdiction or a valid administrative or congressional subpoena, provided that the receiving party promptly notifies the disclosing party of such event so that the disclosing party may seek an appropriate protective order or waive compliance by the receiving party with the terns of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text in Section 2.a allowed for exceptions to the non-disclosure of Proprietary Information as defined in Section 1. The changed text removes the phrase 'or as otherwise specifically provided herein'. This creates a direct contradiction because Section 1 defines Proprietary Information with specific exceptions (i-vi). By removing the reference to these exceptions in Section 2.a, the changed text now implies that all Proprietary Information is protected without exception, conflicting with the definition and permitted disclosures outlined in the contradicted text (Section 1).

perturbation.original_text: Except in connection with any joint project between Cyberlux and ICT, the receiving party shall not make any use of the disclosing party's Proprietary Information for is own benefit or for the benefit of any other individual, corporation or entity.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party shall not make any use of the disclosing party's Proprietary Information for is own benefit or for the benefit of any other individual, corporation or entity.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the phrase 'Except in connection with any joint project between Cyberlux and ICT', the modified text creates an in-text contradiction with the initial 'WHEREAS' clause, which states that 'Cyberlux and ICT intend to enter into discussions concerning a business relationship whereby ICT will provide Cyberlux with certain services'. If there is a joint project, the original text allows for the use of Proprietary Information, but the modified text prohibits it completely. This makes the purpose of the agreement unclear. What would happen if, as the 'WHEREAS' describes a relationship where a joint project requires the use of the other party's Proprietary information?
perturbation.contradicted_text: WHEREAS, Cyberlux and ICT intend to enter into discussions concerning a business relationship whereby ICT will provide Cyberlux with certain services; NOW, THEREFORE, the parties hereto agree as follows:
perturbation.justification: The original text allowed the receiving party to use Proprietary Information in connection with a joint project between Cyberlux and ICT. The changed text removes this exception, creating a direct conflict with the 'WHEREAS' clause, which states that Cyberlux and ICT intend to enter into discussions concerning a business relationship whereby ICT will provide Cyberlux with certain services. This implies the potential for a joint project requiring the use of proprietary information, which the changed text now prohibits, making the purpose and enforceability of the agreement unclear. Therefore, the changed text creates a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each party represents that such degree of care provides adequate protection for its own proprietary information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party represents that such degree of care is sufficient.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'provides adequate protection for its own proprietary information' to 'is sufficient,' the modified text creates an in-text contradiction because it no longer relates back to the *level* of care each party uses to protect *its own* proprietary information. The original text implies a benchmark based on internal practices, providing a reference point for what is deemed sufficient. Without the reference to 'its own proprietary information', the term 'sufficient' becomes ambiguous and unenforceable, contradicting the overall intent of ensuring reasonable protection of Proprietary Information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each party shall maintain the other party's Proprietary Information with at least the same degree of care each party uses to maintain its own proprietary information. Each party represents that such degree of care is sufficient.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'provides adequate protection for its own proprietary information' with 'is sufficient'. This creates a contradiction with the contradicted text 'Each party shall maintain the other party's Proprietary Information with at least the same degree of care each party uses to maintain its own proprietary information. Each party represents that such degree of care is sufficient.' because the original text clearly ties 'sufficient' to the degree of care used to protect *its own* proprietary information, whereas the changed text removes that specific benchmark. The changed text makes 'sufficient' ambiguous, making it difficult to determine if each party is maintaining the other party's Proprietary Information with the adequate level of care, thus creating a contradiction by omission as defined in the instructions.

perturbation.original_text: Each receiving party acknowledges that the Proprietary Information of the disclosing party is central to the disclosing party's business and was developed by or for the disclosing party at a significant cost. Each receiving party further acknowledges that damages would not be an adequate remedy for any breach of this Agreement by the receiving party or its Representatives and that the disclosing party may obtain injunctive or other equitable relief to remedy or prevent any breach or threatened breach of this Agreement by the receiving party or any of its Representatives. Such remedy shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedy for .any such breach of this Agreement, but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or in equity to the disclosing party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each receiving party acknowledges that damages would not be an adequate remedy for any breach of this Agreement by the receiving party or its Representatives and that the disclosing party may obtain injunctive or other equitable relief to remedy or prevent any breach or threatened breach of this Agreement by the receiving party or any of its Representatives. Such remedy shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedy for .any such breach of this Agreement, but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or in equity to the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the sentence 'Each receiving party acknowledges that the Proprietary Information of the disclosing party is central to the disclosing party's business and was developed by or for the disclosing party at a significant cost,' the modified text weakens the justification for injunctive relief. The original acknowledges the special nature of the information, supporting the need for remedies beyond monetary damages. The absence of this justification contradicts the claim that damages are inadequate, since the reason that damages are inadequate has been removed. Therefore Section 4 no longer have a strong and clear basis on why that 'the disclosing party may obtain injunctive or other equitable relief to remedy or prevent any breach or threatened breach of this Agreement by the receiving party or any of its Representatives'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each receiving party acknowledges that damages would not be an adequate remedy for any breach of this Agreement by the receiving party or its Representatives and that the disclosing party may obtain injunctive or other equitable relief to remedy or prevent any breach or threatened breach of this Agreement by the receiving party or any of its Representatives. Such remedy shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedy for .any such breach of this Agreement, but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or in equity to the disclosing party.
perturbation.justification: The original text contains a clause acknowledging the proprietary information's importance, directly supporting the claim that damages would be an inadequate remedy and justifying injunctive relief. The changed text omits this clause. As a result, the justification for seeking injunctive relief weakens, and the contradicted text, which also states that damages would be an inadequate remedy and allows for injunctive relief, is now unsupported. This creates a contradiction because the modified text removes the initial rationale, undermining the grounds for the remedy described in the contradicted text, making the remedy harder to justify, since the 'why' is missing.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may not be modified except by a writing signed by both parties.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be modified by either party with written notice.
perturbation.explanation: The original text in Section 5.a states 'This Agreement may not be modified except by a writing signed by both parties.' The modification states 'This Agreement may be modified by either party with written notice.' This is a direct contradiction. The original text states that both parties need to consent in writing for any modifications to take place, where as the modification makes it so that the parties can modified the agreement with written notice, without needing for the other party to consent.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Agreement may be modified by either party with written notice.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that modification requires agreement/signature from both parties. The changed text states that modification can be done by either party with written notice. The contradicted text which is the same as the Changed Text, contradicts the Original Text, as it requires only one party to provide written notice for a modification to take place. This is a clear in-text contradiction. The change in Section 5.a directly creates a conflict with the original requirement of both parties' consent by allowing modification with only one party's notice.
